perturbation.original_text: that this agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales and the courts of England and Wales shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims)
perturbation.changed_text: that this agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales and the courts of England and Wales shall have jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims)
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'exclusive' jurisdiction could be interpreted as allowing disputes to be heard in other jurisdictions, potentially disadvantaging the weaker party. The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) in the UK restricts the extent to which parties can exclude or limit liability for breach of contract or negligence. By opening the door to other jurisdictions, it may become difficult to enforce UCTA, as the chosen jurisdiction may not have equivalent protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, Section 3, protects against unreasonable contract terms. By removing the 'exclusive jurisdiction', the agreement allows parties to pursue claims outside England and Wales, potentially circumventing the protections offered by the UCTA.

perturbation.justification: The removal of 'exclusive' jurisdiction in the contract could allow disputes to be heard outside of England and Wales. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this could circumvent the protections offered by the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA). Even though the scraped snippets were not helpful, the other information provided supports the determination that a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Signed Your Name Position CompanyAddress Postcode Telephone NumberBUSINESS SALE NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT (continued) fresh thinking for your businessEDT (Electronic document transmissions) Emails with Original Signatures (not electronic) shall be deemed valid and enforceable in respect of any provisions of this NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement). EDT documents shall be subject to European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC, as applicable. Please note that should you choose to return the NDA via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by post.
perturbation.changed_text: Signed Your Name Position CompanyAddress Postcode Telephone NumberBUSINESS SALE NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT (continued) fresh thinking for your businessEDT (Electronic document transmissions) Emails with Original Signatures (not electronic) shall be deemed valid and enforceable in respect of any provisions of this NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement). Please note that should you choose to return the NDA via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by post.
perturbation.explanation: European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC, also known as the Data Protection Directive, was replaced by the GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) in 2018. By omitting the reference to the GDPR, the contract creates legal ambiguity and potential non-compliance regarding the handling of personal data transmitted via email, conflicting with current data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 5 outlines principles relating to processing of personal data. By not referencing GDPR, the contract fails to acknowledge the lawful, fair, and transparent processing of personal data acquired by EDT, potentially violating GDPR requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) – Legal Text Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Welcome to gdpr-info.eu. OJ L 127, 23.5.2018 as a neatly arranged website. All Articles of the GDPR are linked with suitable recitals. The European Data Protection Regulation is applicable as of May 25th, 2018 in all member states to harmonize data privacy laws across Europe. If you find the page useful, feel free to support us by sharing the project. perturbation.justification: The omission of reference to the GDPR, which replaced European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC, creates a legal contradiction. The changed text fails to acknowledge the current data protection laws, specifically the GDPR, which is now the relevant legal standard for handling personal data. The scraped law snippet 2 explicitly states: 'The European Data Protection Regulation is applicable as of May 25th, 2018 in all member states to harmonize data privacy laws across Europe.' By not referencing the GDPR, the contract is not aligned with the applicable law and can potentially violate GDPR requirements.

perturbation.original_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the phrase '(save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information)', the agreement now requires the recipient to destroy confidential information even if legally obligated to retain it. This directly contradicts legal and regulatory requirements for record retention, potentially leading to legal liability for the recipient.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 386 of the Companies Act 2006 requires companies to keep adequate accounting records. The modified NDA clause requiring destruction of information, without exception for legal obligations, directly contradicts this record-keeping requirement.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the exception for legal obligations to retain confidential information. The original text specifically allowed for retaining copies if required by law, enactment, regulation, or stock exchange rules. By omitting this exception, the revised clause mandates the destruction of all confidential information without regard to any legal obligations to retain it, directly contradicting the Companies Act 2006's record-keeping requirements, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Companies Act 2006, reinforcing the legal basis for the record-keeping requirement. Therefore, the omission creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: J) that this agreement does not create any right enforceable by any person who is not a party to it and that the Contracts (Right of Third Parties) Act 1999 as amended shall not apply.
perturbation.changed_text: J) that this agreement does not create any right enforceable by any person who is not a party to it.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause explicitly excludes the application of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. By omitting this, the contract now becomes ambiguous regarding whether third parties can enforce rights under it. While the Act allows for such exclusion, its omission here could create uncertainty and potential legal challenges, especially if a third party could arguably benefit from the NDA. This could undermine the intent of confidentiality if a third party successfully argues they have rights under the contract.
perturbation.law_citation: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, s. 1
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 allows third parties to enforce terms of a contract if the contract expressly provides that they may, or if the term purports to confer a benefit on them. Omitting the explicit exclusion of this act means it is unclear whether third parties could claim rights under this NDA.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly excludes the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, while the changed text omits this exclusion. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that this omission creates uncertainty about whether third parties can claim rights under the NDA. The scraped law snippet, although incomplete, mentions the "Commonwealth Development Corporation Act 1999," suggesting the existence of legislation related to third-party rights, albeit potentially different from the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 mentioned in the explanations. The omission undermines the original intention to prevent third parties from enforcing the agreement, making the contract ambiguous and potentially creating a legal loophole, which can be argued as a contradiction.
